schools.summary: The people demanding gods in Moses' absence: 'and when the people saw that Moses delayed to come down out of the mount, the people gathered themselves together unto Aaron, and said unto him, Up, make us gods, which shall go before us; for as for this Moses, the man that brought us up out of the land of Egypt, we wot not what is become of him' — the people, impatient at Moses' long absence (the forty days, Exodus 24:18), gathering to Aaron and demanding gods to go before them (the impatience, the people unable to wait, distrusting, the demand, the call for visible gods, the breach of the covenant lately made, Exodus 24:3-8); the demand for 'gods' (visible gods, a god they could see, to lead them) — the sin (the breach of the first and second commandments, Exodus 20:3-5, lately given, the people craving a visible deity, the idolatry of the heart breaking out); 'as for this Moses, the man that brought us up out of the land of Egypt, we wot not what is become of him' (the people's contempt for Moses, 'this Moses,' the man, slighting him, and their forgetting that God, not Moses, brought them up, the ingratitude and unbelief, the people turning from God and his servant, the providence, the great apostasy, the golden calf, vv.4) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And when the people saw that Moses delayed to come down out of the mount... Up, make us gods, which shall go before us
the people demanding gods in Moses' absence
the impatient idolatry, the people unable to wait, craving a visible god (Exodus 20:3-5)
the breach of the covenant lately made, the great apostasy begun (Exodus 24:3-8)

schools.summary: Aaron calling for the gold: 'and Aaron said unto them, Break off the golden earrings, which are in the ears of your wives, of your sons, and of your daughters, and bring them unto me' — Aaron, yielding to the people, telling them to break off the golden earrings (from their wives and children) and bring them to him (to make the calf, vv.4, the yielding, Aaron weakly complying, not resisting the sin, the gold, the earrings, the ornaments, for the idol); Aaron's weakness (yielding to the people's demand, not standing firm against the idolatry) — the failure of the leader (Aaron, left in charge, Exodus 24:14, failing to resist, some note he may have hoped the cost of the gold would deter them, or temporized, but he sinned in yielding); 'the golden earrings... and bring them unto me' (the gold collected, for the idol, the providence, the ornaments, perhaps the spoil of Egypt, Exodus 12:35, now turned to idolatry, the sin, the gold given to make the calf) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, JFB, Wesley.

themes: And Aaron said unto them, Break off the golden earrings, which are in the ears of your wives, of your sons, and of your daughters, and bring them unto me
Aaron calling for the gold
Aaron weakly yielding to the people's idolatry, the failure of the leader (Exodus 24:14)
the gold (perhaps the spoil of Egypt) turned to idolatry (Exodus 12:35)

schools.summary: The people bringing their gold for the idol: 'and all the people brake off the golden earrings which were in their ears, and brought them unto Aaron' — the people readily breaking off their earrings and bringing them to Aaron for the idol (the eagerness, the people willing, even eager, to give for their idolatry, the giving, the gold brought, for the calf, vv.4, the zeal for the sin, the people more ready to give for the idol than for God's house); the people readily giving (breaking off their gold for the idol) — the eagerness (the zeal for idolatry, the contrast, some note, with the later freewill offering for the tabernacle, Exodus 35); 'brought them unto Aaron' (the gold brought to Aaron, for the calf, the sin, the people furnishing their own idolatry, the providence, the gold given, the calf to be made, vv.4, the lesson, the readiness of the carnal heart to give for its idols) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And all the people brake off the golden earrings which were in their ears, and brought them unto Aaron
the people bringing their gold for the idol
the eager idolatry, the people willing to give for their sin
the readiness of the carnal heart to give for its idols (contrast Exodus 35)

schools.summary: The making of the golden calf: 'and he received them at their hand, and fashioned it with a graving tool, after he had made it a molten calf: and they said, These be thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt' — Aaron melting the gold and fashioning a molten calf (an image, probably after the Egyptian Apis or Mnevis, the bull-gods of Egypt, the molten calf, the idol, made of the gold, the Egyptian influence, the people turning to the calf-worship of Egypt, from which they had come); the great sin — 'These be thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt': the people ascribing to the calf the deliverance from Egypt (the glory of God given to an idol, the witnesses noting the lesson — the great sin of idolatry, the breach of the first and second commandments, Exodus 20:3-5, lately given, the people not perhaps meaning to forsake the LORD wholly, but to worship him under a visible image, the calf as a representation of God, the will-worship, the corrupting of God's worship with images, the glory of God given to an idol, the gross sin, 'they changed their glory into the similitude of an ox that eateth grass,' Psalm 106:19-20, 'they made a calf in Horeb, and worshipped the molten image,' the New Testament citing this as the great example of idolatry, Acts 7:40-41, Stephen; 1 Corinthians 10:7, 'neither be ye idolaters, as were some of them; as it is written, The people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play,' the apostasy of the heart, the turning from the unseen God to a visible idol); the molten calf, 'These be thy gods' — the great apostasy (the people worshipping the work of their own hands, the providence, the sin that brought God's wrath, vv.7-10, the great fall, lesson for all against idolatry) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What is the nature of the golden calf sin?

sides.position: The great sin of idolatry - the glory of God given to an idol (Psalm 106:19-20), the breach of the first and second commandments lately given (Exodus 20:3-5), the corrupting of God's worship with a visible image; the great New Testament example of idolatry (Acts 7:40-41; 1 Corinthians 10:7), the apostasy of the heart turning from the unseen God

themes: And he received them at their hand, and fashioned it with a graving tool, after he had made it a molten calf: and they said, These be thy gods, O Israel, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt
the making of the golden calf (the crux of the great idolatry)
the glory of God given to an idol, the breach of the first two commandments (Exodus 20:3-5; Psalm 106:19-20)
the great New Testament example of idolatry, the warning to all (Acts 7:40-41; 1 Corinthians 10:7)

schools.summary: Aaron building an altar and proclaiming a feast: 'and when Aaron saw it, he built an altar before it; and Aaron made proclamation, and said, To morrow is a feast to the LORD' — Aaron, seeing the calf (and the people's response), building an altar before it and proclaiming a feast 'to the LORD' (Jehovah, the altar, for the worship of the calf, the proclamation, the feast announced, the worship organized); the feast proclaimed 'to the LORD' (to Jehovah) — the confusion (Aaron mixing the worship of the true God with the idol, calling the calf-feast a feast to Jehovah, the lesson, the corrupting of God's worship, the will-worship that joins idolatry to the name of God, the very sin condemned, the LORD not to be worshipped under an image, Deuteronomy 4:15-18, the syncretism, the calf called a feast to the LORD, the gross confusion); the altar and the feast — the worship of the calf organized (the providence, the idolatry deepening, the feast to follow, vv.6, the sin, Aaron leading the people in the corrupt worship) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And when Aaron saw it, he built an altar before it; and Aaron made proclamation, and said, To morrow is a feast to the LORD
Aaron building an altar and proclaiming a feast
the worship of the calf called a feast to Jehovah, the gross confusion
the LORD not to be worshipped under an image (Deuteronomy 4:15-18)

schools.summary: The idolatrous feast and revelry: 'and they rose up early on the morrow, and offered burnt offerings, and brought peace offerings; and the people sat down to eat and to drink, and rose up to play' — the people rising early, offering burnt and peace offerings (to the calf), then sitting to eat and drink (the sacrificial feast), and rising up to play (revelry, dancing, perhaps lewd sport, the idolatrous worship, the offerings to the calf, the feast, the eating and drinking of the sacrifices); the 'play' — the people rising up to play (Hebrew, to sport, dance, make merry, perhaps with lewd, licentious revelry, the idolatrous feast degenerating into riot and immorality, the witnesses noting the New Testament citing — Paul warning against idolatry and its revelry by this very verse: 'neither be ye idolaters, as were some of them; as it is written, The people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play,' 1 Corinthians 10:7, the lesson, idolatry and sensuality joined, the feast of the idol leading to riot, the warning against the lusts and revelry that attend false worship); the eating, drinking, and play — the idolatrous riot (the feast and the revelry, the sensuality, the providence, the people deep in sin while Moses was on the mount, the wrath of God kindled, vv.7-10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And they rose up early on the morrow, and offered burnt offerings, and brought peace offerings; and the people sat down to eat and to drink, and rose up to play
the idolatrous feast and revelry
idolatry and sensuality joined, the feast leading to riot
the warning against the revelry that attends false worship (1 Corinthians 10:7)

schools.summary: God sending Moses down for the people's corruption: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Go, get thee down; for thy people, which thou broughtest out of the land of Egypt, have corrupted themselves' — God telling Moses to go down, for the people had corrupted themselves (committed the great sin of the calf, the sending-down, Moses called from the mount to the crisis, the corruption, the people corrupted, the sin done, vv.8); God calling them 'thy people' (Moses', not 'my people') — the disowning (God, as it were, disowning the apostate people, putting them off on Moses, the sign of his displeasure, the people forfeiting their place as God's people by their sin, the test, perhaps a trial of Moses' intercession, God speaking as ready to cast them off); 'have corrupted themselves' (the people corrupt, the great sin, the breach of the covenant, the providence, the sin reported, the wrath to be revealed, vv.10, the occasion of Moses' intercession) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Go, get thee down; for thy people, which thou broughtest out of the land of Egypt, have corrupted themselves
God sending Moses down for the people's corruption
God disowning the apostate people, calling them 'thy people' not 'my people'
the great sin reported, the wrath at hand (vv.10)

schools.summary: The swiftness and grossness of the apostasy: 'they have turned aside quickly out of the way which I commanded them: they have made them a molten calf, and have worshipped it, and have sacrificed thereunto, and said, These be thy gods, O Israel, which have brought thee up out of the land of Egypt' — the people having turned aside quickly (soon, swiftly, only days after the covenant, Exodus 24) from the way (the commandments) God gave (the swiftness, the speedy apostasy, how quickly they forgot and fell, the way commanded, the law lately given, Exodus 20-24); the gross sin (the calf made, worshipped, sacrificed to) — the full apostasy (the making, the worship, the sacrifice, the proclamation, the depth of the fall); 'These be thy gods, O Israel, which have brought thee up out of the land of Egypt' (the ascribing of the deliverance to the idol, the glory of God given to the calf, Psalm 106:19-20, the blasphemy, the LORD's work given to an image, the providence, the great sin, the wrath to come, vv.10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: They have turned aside quickly out of the way which I commanded them: they have made them a molten calf, and have worshipped it... These be thy gods, O Israel
the swiftness and grossness of the apostasy
the speedy fall, only days after the covenant (Exodus 24)
the glory of God given to the idol, the blasphemy (Psalm 106:19-20)

schools.summary: God's charge that the people are stiffnecked: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, I have seen this people, and, behold, it is a stiffnecked people' — God declaring the people 'stiffnecked' (stubborn, obstinate, unyielding, like an ox that will not bend its neck to the yoke, the charge, the people's obstinacy, their stubbornness in sin, the stiffneckedness, the persistent rebellion, the hard, unbending heart, Acts 7:51, 'ye stiffnecked... ye do always resist the Holy Ghost'); God's seeing the people (knowing their hearts, their obstinacy) — the divine knowledge (God sees the heart, the stubbornness, the just charge); 'it is a stiffnecked people' (the stiffnecked people, stubborn, obstinate, unbending in sin, Deuteronomy 9:13; Acts 7:51, the providence, the charge that justified the wrath, vv.10, the obstinate people, the occasion of Moses' intercession) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, I have seen this people, and, behold, it is a stiffnecked people
God's charge that the people are stiffnecked
the stubborn, unbending people, persistent in rebellion (Deuteronomy 9:13)
the hard heart that resists the Holy Ghost (Acts 7:51)

schools.summary: God's wrath and the testing offer to Moses: 'now therefore let me alone, that my wrath may wax hot against them, and that I may consume them: and I will make of thee a great nation' — God declaring his wrath would burn against the people to consume them, and offering to make of Moses a great nation instead (the wrath, the just anger against the apostasy, the threatened destruction, the people deserving consuming); 'let me alone' — the remarkable word (God, as it were, asking Moses to let him alone, as if Moses' intercession could hold his hand, the witnesses noting the meaning — God thereby inviting and prompting Moses' intercession, the 'let me alone' implying that Moses' prayer could prevail, that God left room for the mediator's plea, the door opened for intercession, the very saying stirring Moses to pray, the power of intercessory prayer, that God will be entreated, Genesis 18:23-33, Abraham; Ezekiel 22:30, 'I sought for a man... that should stand in the gap,' the test of Moses, whether he would seek his own greatness or the people's good); 'I will make of thee a great nation' (the offer to make Moses a great nation, as Abraham, the promise transferred to Moses — the test of Moses, whether he would accept his own exaltation or intercede for the people, the providence, Moses passing the test, refusing his own greatness, interceding for Israel, vv.11-13, the figure of the selfless mediator) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

sides.position: God inviting and prompting Moses' intercession - the 'let me alone' implying the mediator's prayer could prevail, the door opened for intercession (Genesis 18:23-33; Ezekiel 22:30); and the offer to make Moses a great nation a test, whether he would seek his own greatness or the people's good, which he passed by interceding

themes: Now therefore let me alone, that my wrath may wax hot against them, and that I may consume them: and I will make of thee a great nation
God's wrath and the testing offer to Moses
'let me alone' inviting the mediator's intercession (Genesis 18:23-33; Ezekiel 22:30)
the test of Moses, whether to seek his own greatness or the people's good, which he passed by interceding

schools.summary: Moses beginning his intercession for the people: 'and Moses besought the LORD his God, and said, LORD, why doth thy wrath wax hot against thy people, which thou hast brought forth out of the land of Egypt with great power, and with a mighty hand?' — Moses, instead of accepting his own exaltation (vv.10), interceding for the people, beseeching the LORD to turn from his wrath (the intercession, Moses standing in the gap, pleading for the people, the selfless mediator, the prayer, Moses beginning his plea, vv.11-13); Moses' plea — 'thy people' (turning God's word back, vv.7, 'thy people' Moses', now 'thy people' God's, Moses pleading God's own relation to them) — the argument (the people God's own, redeemed by him); 'which thou hast brought forth out of the land of Egypt with great power, and with a mighty hand' (Moses pleading God's great work of redemption, the people brought out by God's power, not to be destroyed now, the argument, God's own glory and work, the redemption not to be undone, the providence, Moses' intercession, the plea of God's relation and work, vv.12-13) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Moses besought the LORD his God, and said, LORD, why doth thy wrath wax hot against thy people, which thou hast brought forth out of the land of Egypt with great power, and with a mighty hand?
Moses beginning his intercession for the people
the selfless mediator standing in the gap, refusing his own exaltation (vv.10)
pleading God's own relation and redemption (the people brought out by God's hand)

schools.summary: Moses pleading God's glory among the nations: 'wherefore should the Egyptians speak, and say, For mischief did he bring them out, to slay them in the mountains, and to consume them from the face of the earth? Turn from thy fierce wrath, and repent of this evil against thy people' — Moses pleading that, if God destroyed the people, the Egyptians would blaspheme, saying God brought them out only to slay them (for mischief, the argument from God's glory, God's name among the nations, not to be reproached, Numbers 14:13-16; Deuteronomy 9:28, the honor of God's name the great plea); the plea from God's glory (the Egyptians not to blaspheme, God's name not to be reproached) — the argument (the honor of God's name, the great motive in prayer, Psalm 79:9-10; Ezekiel 36:22-23); 'Turn from thy fierce wrath, and repent of this evil against thy people' (the petition, turn from the wrath, relent of the threatened evil, the plea for mercy, Moses asking God to spare, the providence, the prayer that God would answer, vv.14, 'the LORD repented') — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Wherefore should the Egyptians speak... Turn from thy fierce wrath, and repent of this evil against thy people
Moses pleading God's glory among the nations
the honor of God's name the great plea, the Egyptians not to blaspheme (Numbers 14:13-16)
asking God to relent of the threatened evil (Ezekiel 36:22-23)

schools.summary: Moses pleading the covenant with the fathers: 'remember Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, thy servants, to whom thou swarest by thine own self, and saidst unto them, I will multiply your seed as the stars of heaven, and all this land that I have spoken of will I give unto your seed, and they shall inherit it for ever' — Moses pleading the covenant and oath God made with the patriarchs (the promise of the seed and the land, Genesis 12:7; 15:5; 22:16-17; 26:4; 28:13-14, the argument from the covenant, God's own oath, sworn by himself, not to be broken, the unchanging promise, the covenant to the fathers, the ground of the plea); God's oath (sworn by himself, the surest pledge, Hebrews 6:13, 'because he could swear by no greater, he sware by himself') — the immutability of the oath (God's covenant sworn, sure, not to be broken, the strong plea); 'I will multiply your seed... and all this land... will I give unto your seed... for ever' (the promise of the seed and the land, Genesis 15:5; 17:8, the plea, the destruction of the people would break the promise, the providence, Moses pleading the covenant, the sure ground of intercession, God's own oath, the prayer answered, vv.14) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: Remember Abraham, Isaac, and Israel, thy servants, to whom thou swarest by thine own self... I will multiply your seed as the stars of heaven
Moses pleading the covenant with the fathers
God's own oath, sworn by himself, the surest pledge (Genesis 22:16-17; Hebrews 6:13)
the sure ground of intercession, the covenant that cannot be broken (Genesis 15:5)

schools.summary: The LORD repenting in answer to intercession: 'and the LORD repented of the evil which he thought to do unto his people' — the great word — 'the LORD repented of the evil which he thought to do unto his people': God, in answer to Moses' intercession, repenting (relenting, turning from) the threatened destruction (the witnesses weighing the meaning — God's 'repenting' not a change in his eternal counsel, for God is not a man, that he should repent, Numbers 23:19; 1 Samuel 15:29; 'with whom is no variableness,' James 1:17, but a change in his dealing, spoken after the manner of men, an anthropopathism, God, who had purposed to try Moses and to answer his prayer, relenting of the threatened evil in response to the intercession he himself had prompted, vv.10, 'let me alone,' the lesson — the power of intercessory prayer, the prevailing prayer of the mediator, 'the effectual fervent prayer of a righteous man availeth much,' James 5:16, Moses' prayer turning away the wrath, Psalm 106:23, 'he said that he would destroy them, had not Moses his chosen stood before him in the breach, to turn away his wrath,' the mercy of God, ready to be entreated, to turn from wrath at the prayer of the mediator, the figure of Christ's intercession, the great Mediator's prayer prevailing, turning away wrath from his people); 'his people' (now again 'his people,' not cast off, the people spared) — the mercy (the people spared, the wrath turned away, the prayer answered, the providence, the destruction averted, the people preserved through the mediator's prayer) — Wesley, JFB, Gill.

debates.question: How is God's 'repenting' to be understood?

sides.position: Not a change in God's eternal counsel (Numbers 23:19; 1 Samuel 15:29; James 1:17) but his relenting, spoken after the manner of men, in answer to the intercession he himself prompted - the power of the mediator's prevailing prayer (James 5:16; Psalm 106:23, Moses standing in the breach), a figure of Christ's intercession turning away wrath

themes: And the LORD repented of the evil which he thought to do unto his people
the LORD repenting in answer to intercession (the crux of prevailing prayer)
not a change in God's counsel (Numbers 23:19) but his relenting at the mediator's prayer
the power of intercessory prayer (James 5:16; Psalm 106:23), a figure of Christ's intercession

schools.summary: Moses descending with the tables of the testimony: 'and Moses turned, and went down from the mount, and the two tables of the testimony were in his hand: the tables were written on both their sides; on the one side and on the other were they written' — Moses coming down from the mount carrying the two stone tables of the testimony (the Ten Commandments, vv.16, the tables, the law written by God, Exodus 31:18, the writing on both sides, the commandments written on both faces of the tables, full, complete); the two tables (the law, the testimony) in Moses' hand — the divine law (written by God, vv.16, the covenant document); 'the tables were written on both their sides; on the one side and on the other were they written' (the writing on both sides, the law full, complete, written by God on both faces, the providence, the law of God, written by his finger, brought down to the people who had broken it, the contrast, the holy law and the apostate people) — Henry, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Moses turned, and went down from the mount, and the two tables of the testimony were in his hand: the tables were written on both their sides
Moses descending with the tables of the testimony
the law of God, written by his finger (Exodus 31:18), brought down
the holy law brought to the apostate people who had broken it

schools.summary: The tables the work and writing of God: 'and the tables were the work of God, and the writing was the writing of God, graven upon the tables' — the tables themselves God's work (made or prepared by God, not by man), and the writing God's own writing (graven by the finger of God, Exodus 31:18, the divine authorship, the law God's own work and writing, not man's, the permanence, graven in stone, by God); the tables God's work (the first tables, made or prepared by God) — the divine origin (the law from God, not man); 'the writing was the writing of God, graven upon the tables' (the writing God's own, the law written by the finger of God, Exodus 31:18; Deuteronomy 9:10, the figure, the law written by God on stone, the figure of the law written by the Spirit on the heart in the new covenant, Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 3:3, 'written not with ink, but with the Spirit of the living God; not in tables of stone, but in fleshy tables of the heart,' the providence, the divine law, God's own work and writing, soon to be broken by Moses, vv.19, as the people had broken it) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And the tables were the work of God, and the writing was the writing of God, graven upon the tables
the tables the work and writing of God
the law God's own work and writing, graven by his finger (Exodus 31:18)
the figure of the law written by the Spirit on the heart (Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 3:3)

schools.summary: Joshua hearing the noise as war: 'and when Joshua heard the noise of the people as they shouted, he said unto Moses, There is a noise of war in the camp' — Joshua (who had been with Moses, or part way up the mount, Exodus 24:13), hearing the loud noise of the people's revelry, thinking it the noise of war (battle in the camp, the noise, the loud shouting of the idolatrous feast, vv.6, the mistake, Joshua taking the revelry for the sound of war); the loud noise (the shouting of the feast, vv.18) — the revelry (the clamor); 'There is a noise of war in the camp' (Joshua's mistake, taking the noise for war, the providence, the noise of the idolatrous riot, soon to be explained, vv.18, the loud revelry of the apostasy) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And when Joshua heard the noise of the people as they shouted, he said unto Moses, There is a noise of war in the camp
Joshua hearing the noise as war
the loud shouting of the idolatrous feast (vv.6)
Joshua mistaking the clamor of the revelry for the sound of war

schools.summary: Moses discerning the noise as singing: 'and he said, It is not the voice of them that shout for mastery, neither is it the voice of them that cry for being overcome: but the noise of them that sing do I hear' — Moses correcting Joshua: it was not the sound of war (neither the shout of victory nor the cry of defeat), but the noise of singing (the revelry of the idolatrous feast, the discernment, Moses knowing, from God, the true cause, the apostasy, the singing, the noise of the feast, the antiphonal singing or revelry); Moses' correction (not war, neither victory nor defeat) — the discernment (the true cause known, the apostasy, revealed by God, vv.7-8); 'the noise of them that sing do I hear' (the noise of singing, the idolatrous revelry, the providence, Moses knowing the sin, descending to the crisis, vv.19, the contrast, the holy law in his hand, the idolatrous singing below) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And he said, It is not the voice of them that shout for mastery, neither is it the voice of them that cry for being overcome: but the noise of them that sing do I hear
Moses discerning the noise as singing
Moses knowing the true cause, the apostasy revealed by God (vv.7-8)
the noise of the idolatrous feast, the contrast with the holy law in his hand

schools.summary: Moses breaking the tables in anger: 'and it came to pass, as soon as he came nigh unto the camp, that he saw the calf, and the dancing: and Moses' anger waxed hot, and he cast the tables out of his hands, and brake them beneath the mount' — Moses, seeing the calf and the dancing, filled with hot (holy) anger, and casting down and breaking the tables (the sight, the calf and the revelry, the anger, the holy indignation of Moses at the apostasy); Moses' hot anger (the holy, righteous indignation at the great sin) — the zeal (the just wrath, not sinful passion, but holy indignation, as God's, vv.10); the breaking of the tables — the witnesses weighing the act: not rash or sinful (some say), but a deliberate, significant act — the breaking of the tables signifying that the people, by their idolatry, had broken the covenant (the tables, the covenant document, broken because the covenant was broken, the figure, the broken covenant, the law violated, the people forfeiting the covenant by their sin, the providence, the broken tables, the broken covenant, the occasion of the renewal, ch. 34) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass, as soon as he came nigh unto the camp, that he saw the calf, and the dancing: and Moses' anger waxed hot, and he cast the tables out of his hands, and brake them beneath the mount
Moses breaking the tables in anger
the holy indignation of Moses at the apostasy
the broken tables signifying the people's broken covenant (ch. 34)

schools.summary: The destroying of the calf and the drinking: 'and he took the calf which they had made, and burnt it in the fire, and ground it to powder, and strawed it upon the water, and made the children of Israel drink of it' — Moses utterly destroying the calf (burning it, grinding it to powder, scattering it on the water) and making the people drink it (the destruction, the idol utterly destroyed, reduced to nothing, the contempt shown for the worthless idol, the drinking, the people made to drink the dust of their idol, the shame and the punishment); the utter destruction (the idol burned, ground, scattered) — the contempt (the idol shown to be nothing, the worthless god destroyed, Deuteronomy 9:21; Isaiah 44:9-20, the vanity of idols, the thoroughness, no trace of the idol left); 'strawed it upon the water, and made the children of Israel drink of it' (the people made to drink the dust of the idol, the shame, the punishment, the swallowing of their own sin, the figure, the people made to taste the bitterness of their idolatry, perhaps as the bitter water of jealousy, Numbers 5:24, the providence, the idol destroyed, the people shamed, the sin made bitter to them) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, JFB, Wesley.

themes: And he took the calf which they had made, and burnt it in the fire, and ground it to powder, and strawed it upon the water, and made the children of Israel drink of it
the destroying of the calf and the drinking
the idol utterly destroyed, shown to be nothing (Deuteronomy 9:21; Isaiah 44:9-20)
the people made to drink the bitterness of their sin (Numbers 5:24)

schools.summary: Moses confronting Aaron for the great sin: 'and Moses said unto Aaron, What did this people unto thee, that thou hast brought so great a sin upon them?' — Moses confronting Aaron, asking what the people had done to him (what provocation or compulsion) that he had led them into so great a sin (the confrontation, Aaron held responsible, as the leader who yielded, the great sin, the idolatry, the heinous transgression); Moses holding Aaron responsible (the leader who should have resisted) — the accountability (Aaron, left in charge, Exodus 24:14, failing, leading the people into sin, the gravity, the leader's sin in yielding); 'that thou hast brought so great a sin upon them' (the great sin, the idolatry, the heinous transgression brought on the people by Aaron's yielding, the providence, Aaron's responsibility, the leader's guilt, the great sin, the warning to those in authority not to lead others into sin) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And Moses said unto Aaron, What did this people unto thee, that thou hast brought so great a sin upon them?
Moses confronting Aaron for the great sin
Aaron held responsible, the leader who yielded (Exodus 24:14)
the warning to those in authority not to lead others into sin

schools.summary: Aaron's excuse, blaming the people: 'and Aaron said, Let not the anger of my lord wax hot: thou knowest the people, that they are set on mischief' — Aaron, instead of confessing, making excuse, blaming the people (they were set on mischief, evil, prone to it, the excuse, Aaron shifting the blame to the people, minimizing his own guilt, the blaming, the people charged, Aaron excusing himself); Aaron seeking to appease Moses (not provoking, deflecting) — the excuse (the self-justification); 'thou knowest the people, that they are set on mischief' (Aaron blaming the people, their evil disposition, the excuse, shifting the blame, not owning his own sin, the lesson, the way of the guilty, excusing self and blaming others, as Adam, Genesis 3:12, the providence, Aaron's weak excuse, the leader's failure to confess) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Aaron said, Let not the anger of my lord wax hot: thou knowest the people, that they are set on mischief
Aaron's excuse, blaming the people
Aaron shifting the blame, excusing himself, not owning his sin
the way of the guilty, as Adam (Genesis 3:12)

schools.summary: Aaron repeating the people's demand: 'for they said unto me, Make us gods, which shall go before us: for as for this Moses, the man that brought us up out of the land of Egypt, we wot not what is become of him' — Aaron repeating the people's demand (vv.1) as his excuse (they pressed him, demanded gods, the excuse, Aaron citing the people's pressure to justify himself, the demand, the people's words, vv.1, repeated); Aaron citing the people's demand (the pressure) — the excuse (the deflection); 'as for this Moses, the man that brought us up out of the land of Egypt, we wot not what is become of him' (the people's words, the contempt for Moses, the impatience, vv.1, the excuse, Aaron repeating their words, not owning his fault, the providence, Aaron's weak excuse, citing the people's pressure) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: For they said unto me, Make us gods, which shall go before us: for as for this Moses... we wot not what is become of him
Aaron repeating the people's demand
Aaron citing the people's pressure to justify himself (vv.1)
not owning his fault, deflecting the guilt

schools.summary: Aaron's evasive account of the calf: 'and I said unto them, Whosoever hath any gold, let them break it off. So they gave it me: then I cast it into the fire, and there came out this calf' — Aaron giving an evasive, almost absurd account: he cast the gold into the fire, and 'there came out this calf' (as if the calf made itself, minimizing his own act of fashioning it, vv.4, the evasion, Aaron's account false or evasive, hiding his deliberate making of the idol, the absurdity, the calf described as coming out of itself, the excuse foolish and dishonest); Aaron's evasive account (the calf 'came out,' as if of itself, not owning that he fashioned it with a graving tool, vv.4) — the dishonesty (the excuse false, hiding his guilt, the lesson, the way of the guilty, evading and excusing, the lie added to the sin); Aaron's foolish, evasive account — the guilt hidden, the excuse absurd (the providence, the leader's failure, the evasion compounding the sin, the warning against excusing and hiding sin) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And I said unto them, Whosoever hath any gold, let them break it off. So they gave it me: then I cast it into the fire, and there came out this calf
Aaron's evasive account of the calf
the absurd excuse, the calf as if it made itself, hiding his deliberate fashioning (vv.4)
the way of the guilty, the lie added to the sin

schools.summary: The people made naked to their shame: 'and when Moses saw that the people were naked; (for Aaron had made them naked unto their shame among their enemies:)' — Moses seeing the people 'naked' (unrestrained, broken loose, perhaps literally undressed in the revelry, or figuratively stripped of their honor, restraint, and God's protection, the nakedness, the people broken loose, riotous, shameless, stripped of restraint, the shame, Aaron's failure leaving them exposed to shame among their enemies); the people naked (broken loose, unrestrained, riotous, perhaps stripped, or stripped of God's protection and their honor) — the disorder (the people out of control, shameless in the idolatry); 'for Aaron had made them naked unto their shame among their enemies' (Aaron's failure exposing the people to shame, their idolatry a reproach among their enemies, the figure, the people stripped of God's favor and protection, exposed, shamed, the providence, the leader's failure, the people exposed, the shame of the apostasy) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And when Moses saw that the people were naked; (for Aaron had made them naked unto their shame among their enemies:)
the people made naked to their shame
the people broken loose, unrestrained, stripped of restraint and protection
shamed by the apostasy, a reproach among their enemies

schools.summary: Moses' call, 'Who is on the LORD's side?': 'then Moses stood in the gate of the camp, and said, Who is on the LORD'S side? let him come unto me. And all the sons of Levi gathered themselves together unto him' — the great call — 'Who is on the LORD'S side? let him come unto me': Moses, standing in the gate of the camp, calling for all who were on the LORD's side to come to him (the witnesses noting the meaning — the decisive call to separate from the apostasy, to take a stand for God against the idolatry, the lesson — the call for decision, the dividing line between the LORD's side and the idolaters, no neutrality, the people must choose, 'choose you this day whom ye will serve,' Joshua 24:15; 'he that is not with me is against me,' Matthew 12:30, the rallying of the faithful, the call to come out and be separate, 2 Corinthians 6:17, the decisive moment of choosing the LORD's side, openly, against the multitude); 'all the sons of Levi gathered themselves together unto him' (the Levites gathering to Moses, the tribe responding to the call, taking their stand for the LORD) — the zeal (the Levites' decision, their stand for God, rewarded with the priesthood, vv.29; Deuteronomy 33:9, 'who said... I have not seen him... they have observed thy word, and kept thy covenant,' the faithful remnant, the Levites coming to the LORD's side); Moses' call, the Levites' gathering — the decisive choice for the LORD (the providence, the call to decision, the separation of the faithful, the stand for God against idolatry) — Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'Who is on the LORD's side?' call for?

sides.position: The decisive call to separate from the apostasy and take a stand for God - the dividing line with no neutrality (Joshua 24:15; Matthew 12:30; 2 Corinthians 6:17), the rallying of the faithful, the Levites responding with zeal and stand for the LORD (Deuteronomy 33:9)

themes: Then Moses stood in the gate of the camp, and said, Who is on the LORD'S side? let him come unto me. And all the sons of Levi gathered themselves together unto him
Moses' call, 'Who is on the LORD's side?' (the crux of the decisive choice)
the decisive call to separate from the apostasy and stand for God, no neutrality (Joshua 24:15; Matthew 12:30)
the Levites responding with zeal and stand for the LORD (Deuteronomy 33:9; 2 Corinthians 6:17)

schools.summary: The charge to the Levites to slay the idolaters: 'and he said unto them, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Put every man his sword by his side, and go in and out from gate to gate throughout the camp, and slay every man his brother, and every man his companion, and every man his neighbour' — the Levites commanded, in God's name, to take their swords and slay the idolaters throughout the camp, even their own brethren and neighbours (the ringleaders, the obstinate, those who would not repent, the command, the judicial execution of the idolaters, by God's authority, the severity, the slaying of the guilty, the purging of the great sin); the command to slay (the judicial punishment of the idolaters, by God's express command) — the justice (the purging of the apostasy, the execution of God's judgment on the obstinate idolaters); 'slay every man his brother... his companion... his neighbour' (the severity, even kindred and friends, the Levites putting God's command above natural ties, Deuteronomy 33:9, 'who said... I knew them not,' the zeal, the Levites' faithfulness in executing the judgment, the providence, the purging of the great sin, the judgment on the idolaters, the warning against apostasy) — Calvin, Gill, Clarke, Wesley.

themes: And he said unto them, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Put every man his sword by his side... and slay every man his brother, and every man his companion, and every man his neighbour
the charge to the Levites to slay the idolaters
the judicial execution of the idolaters by God's command, the purging of the great sin
the Levites putting God's command above natural ties (Deuteronomy 33:9)

schools.summary: The Levites slaying about three thousand: 'and the children of Levi did according to the word of Moses: and there fell of the people that day about three thousand men' — the Levites obeying, slaying about three thousand of the idolaters that day (the obedience, the Levites faithfully executing the judgment, the number, about three thousand, the guilty, the ringleaders, slain, the judgment, the purging of the great sin); the Levites' obedience (executing the command) — the faithfulness (the zeal for God); 'there fell of the people that day about three thousand men' (the three thousand slain, the guilty idolaters, the contrast, the witnesses note, three thousand fell under the law at the giving of the law and the calf, but three thousand were converted and saved at Pentecost when the Spirit and the gospel came, Acts 2:41 — the law slaying, the gospel giving life, 2 Corinthians 3:6, 'the letter killeth, but the spirit giveth life,' the providence, the judgment on the idolaters, the purging of the sin) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the children of Levi did according to the word of Moses: and there fell of the people that day about three thousand men
the Levites slaying about three thousand
the guilty idolaters slain, the judgment executed
the law slaying, in contrast with the three thousand given life at Pentecost (Acts 2:41; 2 Corinthians 3:6)

schools.summary: The consecration of the Levites by their zeal: 'for Moses had said, Consecrate yourselves to day to the LORD, even every man upon his son, and upon his brother; that he may bestow upon you a blessing this day' — Moses telling the Levites that by their zeal (even against their own kindred) they consecrated themselves to the LORD, and would receive a blessing (the consecration, the Levites set apart to God by their faithful zeal, the blessing, the priesthood, the special service of God, given to the Levites, Deuteronomy 33:8-11; Numbers 3; 8); the Levites' zeal (executing the judgment even on their own kindred, putting God above natural ties) — the consecration by zeal (the Levites proving their devotion to God, Deuteronomy 33:9, the faithfulness); 'that he may bestow upon you a blessing this day' (the blessing, the priesthood, the special service, the reward of their zeal, the providence, the Levites consecrated and blessed, set apart for God's service by their faithfulness, the figure of the consecration by zeal for God) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: For Moses had said, Consecrate yourselves to day to the LORD, even every man upon his son, and upon his brother; that he may bestow upon you a blessing this day
the consecration of the Levites by their zeal
the Levites set apart to God by their faithful zeal, even against kindred (Deuteronomy 33:9)
rewarded with the blessing of the priesthood (Deuteronomy 33:8-11)

schools.summary: Moses returning to intercede and make atonement: 'and it came to pass on the morrow, that Moses said unto the people, Ye have sinned a great sin: and now I will go up unto the LORD; peradventure I shall make an atonement for your sin' — Moses charging the people with their great sin, and announcing he would go up to the LORD to seek atonement (forgiveness) for them (the rebuke, the great sin charged home, the intercession, Moses returning to the mount to plead for the people); Moses going up to 'make an atonement' (to seek forgiveness, to intercede, to plead for pardon) — the mediation (Moses the mediator, going to God to seek atonement for the people's sin, the 'peradventure,' the uncertainty, the great sin, yet Moses venturing to plead, the figure, Moses the type of Christ the Mediator, going to God to make atonement for the people's sin, pleading for their forgiveness, Hebrews 7:25; 1 John 2:1-2); Moses going up to intercede and make atonement — the mediation (the selfless mediator, pleading for the guilty, the providence, Moses' intercession, the figure of Christ's atoning mediation, vv.31-32) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass on the morrow, that Moses said unto the people, Ye have sinned a great sin: and now I will go up unto the LORD; peradventure I shall make an atonement for your sin
Moses returning to intercede and make atonement
Moses the mediator going to God to seek atonement for the people's sin
a figure of Christ pleading for the guilty (Hebrews 7:25; 1 John 2:1-2)

schools.summary: Moses confessing the people's great sin: 'and Moses returned unto the LORD, and said, Oh, this people have sinned a great sin, and have made them gods of gold' — Moses returning to the LORD and confessing the people's great sin (the idolatry, the gods of gold), with grief ('Oh,' the confession, Moses owning the great sin before God, not excusing it, the grief, the 'Oh,' the lament over the sin, the honesty, Moses confessing the sin plainly, the gods of gold); Moses' confession (owning the great sin, with grief) — the honest plea (the mediator confessing the sin, not hiding it, the ground of seeking forgiveness); 'and have made them gods of gold' (the sin named, the gods of gold, the idolatry, the calf, the confession, the sin plainly owned, the providence, Moses' confession, the prelude to the great plea, vv.32) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And Moses returned unto the LORD, and said, Oh, this people have sinned a great sin, and have made them gods of gold
Moses confessing the people's great sin
Moses owning the great sin before God, with grief, not excusing it
the honest confession the ground of seeking forgiveness

schools.summary: Moses' offer to be blotted out for the people: 'yet now, if thou wilt forgive their sin—; and if not, blot me, I pray thee, out of thy book which thou hast written' — Moses' plea breaking off ('if thou wilt forgive their sin—', the sentence unfinished, the heart too full, the longing for forgiveness too great to complete, the broken sentence, the deep emotion, the longing for the people's pardon, the plea for forgiveness, the first and great desire); the great word — 'if not, blot me out of thy book': Moses offering, if God would not forgive the people, to be blotted out of God's book (the book of life, or the book of the living) himself, rather than see them perish (the witnesses noting the meaning — the intensity of Moses' love for the people, willing to perish himself for their sake, the selfless mediator, the lesson — the love of the true mediator, willing to be sacrificed for the people, the parallel of Paul, 'I could wish that myself were accursed from Christ for my brethren,' Romans 9:3, and above all, the figure of Christ the true Mediator, who did not merely offer but actually gave himself, bore the curse, was 'blotted out,' made a curse, forsaken, for his people, that they might be forgiven, Galatians 3:13; 2 Corinthians 5:21, the Lamb's book of life and Christ's substitution, the depth of mediatorial love, the type of Christ's atoning self-sacrifice); the 'book' (the book of life, or the register of the living, of God's people) — Moses offering to be struck out of it (the providence, the selfless offer, a figure of Christ who was made a curse for us, God's answer, vv.33, the sinner to bear his own sin, pointing to the need of the true substitute, Christ) — Ellicott, Wesley, JFB, Gill.

debates.question: What does Moses' offer 'blot me out of thy book' signify?

sides.position: The intensity of Moses' love, willing to perish for the people (the selfless mediator, paralleled by Paul, Romans 9:3), and above all a figure of Christ the true Mediator who actually gave himself, bore the curse, was forsaken for his people that they might be forgiven (Galatians 3:13; 2 Corinthians 5:21) - the type of Christ's atoning self-sacrifice

themes: Yet now, if thou wilt forgive their sin—; and if not, blot me, I pray thee, out of thy book which thou hast written
Moses' offer to be blotted out for the people (the crux of the self-offering mediator)
the intensity of Moses' love, willing to perish for the people (paralleled by Paul, Romans 9:3)
a figure of Christ the true Mediator who gave himself and bore the curse for his own (Galatians 3:13; 2 Corinthians 5:21)

schools.summary: God's answer that the sinner bears his own sin: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Whosoever hath sinned against me, him will I blot out of my book' — God answering Moses that the sinner himself (not the innocent Moses) would be blotted out (the principle, each bears his own sin, the guilty punished, not the innocent for the guilty in this case, the answer, Moses' offer not accepted, the sinner to bear his own guilt, Ezekiel 18:4, 'the soul that sinneth, it shall die'); the principle of personal guilt (the sinner bears his own sin, not Moses for them) — the justice (each accountable for his own sin, Ezekiel 18:20; Deuteronomy 24:16, the lesson, Moses could not be the substitute, no mere man can bear another's sin and atone — pointing to the need of the true Substitute, Christ, who alone can and did bear the sin of others); 'him will I blot out of my book' (the guilty blotted out, the sinner punished, the figure, the principle that the sinner bears his own sin, the law's justice, which only Christ the true Substitute could satisfy by bearing the sin of others, Isaiah 53:6; 1 Peter 2:24, the providence, God's answer, the sinner to bear his own guilt, the need of the true Mediator) — Calvin, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Whosoever hath sinned against me, him will I blot out of my book
God's answer that the sinner bears his own sin
the principle of personal guilt - each bears his own sin (Ezekiel 18:4, 20)
no mere man can be the substitute, pointing to the need of Christ the true Substitute (Isaiah 53:6)

schools.summary: God's command to lead on with the Angel, yet visiting the sin: 'therefore now go, lead the people unto the place of which I have spoken unto thee: behold, mine Angel shall go before thee: nevertheless in the day when I visit I will visit their sin upon them' — God commanding Moses to lead the people on toward the promised land, with God's Angel going before them (the command, the journey to continue, the people not utterly cast off, the Angel, the Angel of God's presence, going before them, vv.33:2, the Angel of the covenant, Exodus 23:20-23); the Angel going before (God's presence, though, as the next chapter shows, a question whether God himself or an angel would go, Exodus 33:2-3, 14-15) — the guidance (the Angel leading, the mercy, the people not abandoned, yet the relation strained by the sin); 'in the day when I visit I will visit their sin upon them' (the threatened visitation, the sin not wholly passed over, to be visited in due time, the judgment reserved, the justice, the sin remembered, the consequences to follow, though the people were spared destruction now, the providence, the people led on, yet the sin to be visited, the mingling of mercy and judgment, some note this visited in the later plagues and the deaths in the wilderness) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: Therefore now go, lead the people unto the place of which I have spoken unto thee: behold, mine Angel shall go before thee: nevertheless in the day when I visit I will visit their sin upon them
God's command to lead on with the Angel, yet visiting the sin
the people led on, not abandoned, the Angel going before (Exodus 23:20-23)
the mingling of mercy and judgment, the sin remembered and to be visited

schools.summary: The LORD plaguing the people for the calf: 'and the LORD plagued the people, because they made the calf, which Aaron made' — the LORD sending a plague (some judgment, sickness or death) on the people for the sin of the calf (the plague, the visitation of the sin, vv.34, the judgment, the consequence of the great sin, the people punished, the responsibility, the calf 'which Aaron made,' yet the people's sin, both guilty); the plague (the judgment on the people for the idolatry) — the justice (the sin visited, the consequence, the people, though spared destruction, not wholly unpunished, the sin bearing its fruit); 'which Aaron made' (the calf which Aaron made, Aaron's part in the sin noted, yet the people made it too, vv.1-4, the shared guilt, the leader and the people both responsible, the providence, the plague, the visitation of the sin, the warning against idolatry, the chapter closing on the judgment, the covenant broken, to be renewed, chs. 33-34) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD plagued the people, because they made the calf, which Aaron made
the LORD plaguing the people for the calf
the judgment on the idolatry, the sin bearing its fruit (vv.34)
the shared guilt of leader and people, the warning against apostasy
